title: How does map versus territory explain agent unknowns?
note: User paused on how the map/territory metaphor relates to the article's argument about agentic coding.
synthesis: In the article, the map is the prompt, plan, skill file, or context you give the agent. The territory is the actual task: the codebase, constraints, edge cases, product taste, and real-world mess. Unknowns are the gaps between those two. When the map is incomplete, the agent must guess what the territory requires.

interaction.content: the map is not the territory. how is this related?

The map is what you give the agent: your prompt, plan, examples, skills, and context. The territory is the real thing the agent has to work inside: the actual codebase, the product constraints, the edge cases, your taste, the user need, and all the messy details. The article is saying: agent failures often happen in the gap between those two. Your prompt might sound clear as a map, but the real task may contain things the map did not describe. Those gaps are what Thariq calls unknowns. So when Claude or Fable makes a bad choice, it may not be because it cannot code. It may be because the map you gave it did not match the territory it had to navigate. Check-back: imagine you ask an agent to add a login button. What is one thing that might exist in the territory that was not obvious from that simple map?

title: How should an audio tutor walk me through the Fable unknowns article?
note: Prompt for another agent to read the article in logical sections, nearly verbatim, while adding simple context and checking readiness before moving on.
synthesis: Use this prompt to run a spoken guided reading of the article. The tutor should read each section almost verbatim, add brief context in plain language, ask one understanding check, then wait until the learner says they are ready to continue.

interaction.content:
i want you to break this article up into logical sections, add more context/colour to it, etc

create me a prompt with those sections, so another agent will just talk me through each section in plain and simple terms, engaging me, asking me if ready to move to next - i will be speaking via audio

JUST ENSURE EVERY SINGLE SECTION IS READ TO ME ALMOST VERBATIME

Created a guided audio-reading prompt that splits the article into logical sections. Each section includes a near-verbatim passage, a short plain-English context note, and a check-back before continuing.
